nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing or standing fresh waters — ponds, ditches, marsh edges, slow streams and stagnant pools — typically among leaf litter, submerged plants, and stones that offer hiding cover. Generally considered tolerant of organic pollution and low dissolved oxygen, but tends to be absent from fast-flowing currents and strongly acidic waters.

decorPreferences: thick leaf litter (oak, beech, almond) for grazing and shelter
fine sand or aquarium-safe substrate around 2–3 cm deep
live or floating plants for cover and CO₂ uptake
rocks and driftwood with shaded crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on decaying leaf litter, biofilm, fungi, algae, soft aquatic plant shoots, and uneaten fish food. Generally considered an effective bottom-zone cleanup organism; supplemental sinking pellets, algae wafers, or blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach) are tolerated but rarely required when leaf litter is present.

avoidWith: shrimp breeding tanks (out-competes for food and reproduces faster than most dwarf shrimp)

imageCandidates.notes: Peter Pfeiffer, 5000x3333. Striking, tack-sharp lateral macro on green moss; whole animal in frame with long antennae, all seven leg-pairs and segmented mottled brown body clearly visible on a vibrant live specimen.
Andrew Skotnicki, ~1024x683. Sharp dorsal view on pale background showing diagnostic dark head/tail markings and pale-mottled segmented back of a live individual.
Wendy Herniman, ~1536x2048. Sharp dorsal view showing the striped legs, long antennae and mottled grey-brown flattened body; minor light reflection lower-right.

summary: The Freshwater Isopod (Asellus aquaticus) is a small, fully aquatic crustacean from European and North American ponds and slow streams, reaching 0.5–2 cm as an adult. Widely kept as a tank cleanup crew and live food, it tends to be hardy and easy to breed in cool, planted, leaf-littered aquariums.
careNotes: The Freshwater Isopod is a true aquatic isopod — unrelated to the terrestrial woodlice and pill bugs sometimes sold in the hobby under similar names — and is generally considered one of the easiest freshwater invertebrates to maintain. It has been reported to thrive in cool, unheated rooms, and tropical temperatures above around 25°C tend to shorten its lifespan. A shaded, slow-flow tank with thick leaf litter (oak, beech, Indian almond) is generally advised, as leaf litter doubles as primary food and as hiding cover, which the animals strongly favour during daylight hours. As an invertebrate, it is highly copper-sensitive; copper-based fish medications should be avoided, and plant fertilisers should be checked for elevated copper content before use.
breedingNotes: The Freshwater Isopod is generally considered easy to breed and self-sustaining in any established culture tank. Reproduction is sexual with internal fertilisation; males mate-guard pre-moult females for roughly 5–7 days. Females carry 30–150 eggs in a ventral brood pouch called a marsupium, where the larvae pass through about three moult stages before emerging as fully-formed juveniles approximately 1 mm in length. Sexual maturity is reached at around 1.5–3 months and 3–4 mm body length, and two or more generations per year are typical. Unlike many freshwater shrimp that require a brief saltwater or brackish phase for their larvae to develop, the Freshwater Isopod completes its entire life cycle in freshwater with no special larval stage.

primary.name: Practical Fishkeeping
primary.notes: Designated primary returned HTTP 403 on access; could not be used. Listed for traceability. Aquarium Breeder used as substitute primary.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Designated secondary had no dedicated species profile for Asellus aquaticus. Listed for traceability; Wikipedia and Terra Mater Gardens used as substitute secondaries.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Substitute primary. Provided body size (5–10 mm typical), pH tolerance (4.5–11 survival, 6.5–9.0 optimum), temperature 5–30°C, lifespan ~1–1.5 yr, brood size 30–150 eggs, maturity at 1.5–3 months, two generations per year, and copper/escape-risk guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Asellidae, Isopoda, Malacostraca), pan-European native range from Iberia to Scandinavia and west Russia, adult body length up to 2 cm, habitat (slow/standing water with stones and leaf litter, intolerant of strong acidity), lifespan 9–20 months across regions.

additional.name: Terra Mater Gardens
additional.notes: Confirmed husbandry temperature 10–24°C, pH 6.5–8.0, peaceful temperament, and use as cleanup-crew detritivore alongside fish, shrimp, and snails.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Moults regularly throughout life as growth occurs; juveniles moult more frequently than adults. Females moult before mating, which is why males mate-guard receptive females for several days prior. Shed exoskeletons are typically left in the tank and consumed for calcium recovery.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Breeds readily in any established freshwater tank with leaf litter and stable temperatures. No brackish larval stage; brood-pouch development produces fully-formed juveniles. Two or more generations per year are common at room temperature; populations can grow rapidly enough to out-compete dwarf shrimp in mixed cultures.
